title: Architectural risks of the registry itself

items.title: Registry documents governance rather than product architecture
items.observation: 17 of 23 nodes belong to architecture-governance, security-safety, or workflow-governance. Only 6 nodes describe product capability layers (parser-ingestion, retrieval-embedding, graph-runtime, temporal-model, generated-cypher). The registry currently describes itself more than the product.
items.implication: Successful S04 verifier runs cannot be read as product-architecture validation; they validate that the registry validates itself.
items.mitigation: Add product-layer items (requirements, components, interfaces, data entities) sourced from prd/01_general_idea.md, prd/02_architecture.md, prd/03_PRD.md so product surface is at least minimally represented.
non_claims: Does not assert governance records should be removed.
Does not assert that product coverage will fix verifier scope.

items.title: Heavy coupling to .gsd/ paths outside prd/architecture/
items.observation: Most source anchors point into .gsd/milestones/M001/slices/S0*/..., .gsd/REQUIREMENTS.md, .gsd/DECISIONS.md. If .gsd/ is reorganized, renamed, or moved, the majority of records will fail anchor freshness checks simultaneously.
items.implication: The registry depends on the stability of an external directory whose lifecycle is not described in the architecture contract.
items.mitigation: Document .gsd/ path stability assumptions in README, or add a path-mapping layer that survives renames. Consider treating .gsd/* as 'external-reference' with mirrored selectors.
non_claims: Does not assert .gsd/ will be renamed.
Does not propose a specific path-mapping mechanism.

items.title: Verifier success creates a false sense of architectural correctness
items.observation: scripts/verify-architecture-graph.py is read-only by design and explicitly does not validate product readiness, runtime behavior, parser completeness, retrieval quality, legal-answer correctness, generated-Cypher safety, FalkorDB production scale, or LLM authority. The README states this, but consumers may still equate 'verifier passes' with 'architecture is correct'.
items.implication: Risk that downstream agents quote verifier success as proof of architectural quality, despite the explicit boundary.
items.mitigation: Add a 'what verifier success means' versus 'what it does not mean' summary card to the verifier CLI output and to claims_ledger.md.
non_claims: Does not assert verifier scope must change.
Does not assert any current overclaim by maintainers.

items.title: Schema v1 has no documented evolution path
items.observation: schema_version is locked at legalgraph-architecture-registry/v1 with no migration policy. Future additions (new layers, new edge types, new required fields, see R04-INC-011, R04-INC-015) currently have no rollover plan.
items.implication: Either breaking changes silently land (corrupting older records), or any non-trivial schema upgrade is blocked behind ad-hoc migrations.
items.mitigation: Define a schema evolution policy: parallel-version acceptance window, extractor migration step, verifier behavior on mixed schema_version, deprecation log location.
non_claims: Does not propose a specific compat policy.

items.title: Traceability gap to prd/parser/ artifacts
items.observation: The sibling directory prd/parser/ contains concrete parser smoke records, ODT smoke records, consultant relation candidates, source-fixture inventory, and schemas. The architecture registry has no evidence or component records anchored to these artifacts. S05-PARSER-ODT-BOUNDARY anchors only to .gsd/ summaries.
items.implication: Parser evidence is duplicated between .gsd/ summaries and prd/parser/ artifacts without a registry-side bridge, weakening traceability of parser claims.
items.mitigation: Add evidence items anchored to prd/parser/odt_smoke_records.md, prd/parser/parser_staging_graph.md, prd/parser/consultant_relation_candidates.md and link them via evidenced_by to existing parser records.
non_claims: Does not assert prd/parser/ files are authoritative.
Does not require duplicating their content into the registry.

items.title: Critical product surfaces depend on records that do not exist yet
items.observation: Critical-risk surfaces — generated-Cypher safety, legal-evidence grounding, api-product surface — have proof gates only by absence. The registry can pass verification while leaving these surfaces unmodeled.
items.implication: A 'no records, no gate, no failure' loophole: surfaces with the highest risk can be implicitly accepted because the registry does not assert anything about them.
items.mitigation: Add explicit proof_gate records (proof_level=none, status=active, risk=high/critical) for each critical surface that lacks coverage, so verifier and views surface the gaps.
non_claims: Does not assert these surfaces are currently unsafe.
Does not require implementation of the surfaces themselves.

items.title: Generated views can drift independently from each other
items.observation: architecture_health.md, product_readiness_blockers.md, and claims_ledger.md are produced by a single generator but are evaluated by humans independently. If one view is regenerated and another is not, contradictions can appear without a verifier failure if the bundle freshness gate is bypassed via custom paths.
items.implication: Custom-path runs of the views generator are not strictly invalid but are not registry-current proof; the README warns about this for verifier, less so for views.
items.mitigation: Add an explicit 'views freshness' note in the README clarifying that custom --output-dir runs are diagnostic only and not authoritative.
non_claims: Does not assert that drift currently exists.
Does not require disabling custom-path runs.

items.title: Identifier pattern allows shadow IDs across record kinds
items.observation: Schema identifier pattern ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_-]*-[A-Za-z0-9_.:-]+$ does not separate item IDs from edge IDs by prefix. Current convention (REQ-, DEC-, GATE-, RISK-, EDGE-, ASSUMP-, CHECK-, M00*-, S0*-) is by author discipline only.
items.implication: If a future author creates an item with EDGE- prefix or an edge with REQ- prefix, schema validation will not reject it; downstream tooling that filters by prefix could silently misclassify records.
items.mitigation: Either enforce prefix-by-kind in the schema (with a pattern union) or document the prefix convention as a hard contract and add an extractor check.
non_claims: Does not assert that the current registry contains misclassified records.
Does not require schema change as the only path.
Risks describe the registry's exposure surface, not current product runtime failure.
Mitigations are candidate actions, not committed work.
Does not promote any record into architecture_items.jsonl.
